coredata.dc:description: In the present study, a series of seven carbon dots, either containing or lacking the N element, were successfully synthesized via the one-pot hydrothermal method using green raw materials as carbon source. Among these carbon dots, three of them, which were prepared from Protium serratum shell, Coriandrum sativum and Tamarindus, were found to be devoid of the N element. Notably, these three N-free carbon dots exhibited a more pronounced inhibitory efficiency against human lysozyme amyloid fibrillation in comparison to the other four carbon dots incorporating the N element. XPS analysis revealed that these three carbon dots possessed a relatively high oxygen content, which was presumably the principal factor contributing to their enhanced inhibitory efficiency. All three of these carbon dots were capable of quenching the fluorescence of human lysozyme, and the quenching mechanism was identified as static quenching. Additionally, these three carbon dots also demonstrated favorable antioxidant properties and low cytotoxicity. Specifically, the inhibition ratio of the carbon dots from Protium serratum shell on human lysozyme amyloid fibrillation reached a remarkable value of 92 % at a mass ratio of 5:1 (human lysozyme: carbon dots). The highest binding constant of the carbon dots from Protium serratum shell with the protein could account for its superior inhibition capability. This investigation demonstrated that the carbon dots with a high oxygen content could effectively inhibit amyloid fibrillation, thereby paving the way for the development of multifunctional carbon dots that are capable of synchronously inhibiting protein amyloid fibrillation and scavenging free radicals.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional carbon dots inhibit the amyloid fibrillation and scavenge free radicals

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, a series of seven carbon dots, either containing or lacking the N element, were successfully synthesized via the one-pot hydrothermal method using green raw materials as carbon source. Among these carbon dots, three of them, which were prepared from Protium serratum shell, Coriandrum sativum and Tamarindus, were found to be devoid of the N element. Notably, these three N-free carbon dots exhibited a more pronounced inhibitory efficiency against human lysozyme amyloid fibrillation in comparison to the other four carbon dots incorporating the N element. XPS analysis revealed that these three carbon dots possessed a relatively high oxygen content, which was presumably the principal factor contributing to their enhanced inhibitory efficiency. All three of these carbon dots were capable of quenching the fluorescence of human lysozyme, and the quenching mechanism was identified as static quenching. Additionally, these three carbon dots also demonstrated favorable antioxidant properties and low cytotoxicity. Specifically, the inhibition ratio of the carbon dots from Protium serratum shell on human lysozyme amyloid fibrillation reached a remarkable value of 92 % at a mass ratio of 5:1 (human lysozyme: carbon dots). The highest binding constant of the carbon dots from Protium serratum shell with the protein could account for its superior inhibition capability. This investigation demonstrated that the carbon dots with a high oxygen content could effectively inhibit amyloid fibrillation, thereby paving the way for the development of multifunctional carbon dots that are capable of synchronously inhibiting protein amyloid fibrillation and scavenging free radicals.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Amyloid fibrils, being insoluble protein aggregates, have the propensity to deposit on the tissues and organs in the body, thereby inducing a series of neurodegenerative diseases. [1–3] These toxic aggregates may trigger oxidative stress during the procedure of amyloid fibrillation and, in turn, the oxidative stress could accelerate the progression of amyloid fibrillation. [4–5] Consequently, inhibiting the amyloid fibrillation and scavenging the excess free radicals may serve as effective means to prevent the progression of these diseases. Until now, 37 proteins have been found to be related to neurodegenerative diseases. Among them, human lysozyme (HL) represents a type of a kind of pathogenic protein that can be detected in various tissues and body fluids. [6–7] The variant form of HL is capable of forming amyloid fibrils, and these amyloid fibrils can deposit on diverse organs, such as the spleen, liver and kidney, which could lead to the dysfunction of these organs. [8]

Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel class of zero-dimension nanomaterials, have gained extensive attention due to their excellent performances, such as easy preparation, low cytotoxicity, resistance to photo-bleaching and good biocompatibility. [9–11] They have found wide applications in diverse fields, including in cellular imaging, [12–13] biomedicine [14–15] and cancer therapy fields. [16–17] For example, metformin (Glucophage)-based CDs have been demonstrated to remarkably suppress the growth of colorectal cancer in mice. [18] CDs prepared from genistein exhibited good antioxidant and anti-flammatory properties in vitro. [19] CDs derived from Congo red were capable of simultaneously inhibiting protein amyloid aggregation and scavenging free radicals. [4] Red emissive CDs originating from quercetin could serve as a novel effective multifunctional nano-agent for retarding protein amyloid fibrillation. [20]

Plenty of small molecules and polymers have been employed as precursors for the preparation of CDs, including o-Phenylenediamine, [21] citric acid, [22] poly(poly(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate [23] and so on. Nevertheless, there has been a growing demand for CDs prepared from green precursors. Green precursors are defined as substances that are ubiquitously present in nature, readily accessible, sustainable and environmentally friendly. [24–27] For example, CDs possessing antiviral activities have been obtained by using withania somnifera through a one-pot hydrothermal method. [28] The Gao groups prepared CDs by employing coconut petiole residues as precursors via a simple hydrothermal method for the detection of Fe3+. [29] The utilization of green and sustainable precursors in the preparation of CDs can not only cut down cost but also facilitate the application of sustainable and environmental friendly ideas in the development of numerous fields. [30–33]

Flavonoids represent a common class of polyphenolic compounds that possess antioxidant and anti-fibrillation properties. [34–36] However, their applications are significantly limited because of their poor water-solubility and relatively high toxicity. There are literature reports indicating that using flavonoids as carbon source for the preparation of CDs can enhance their water-solubility. Simultaneously, it can also retain the antioxidant and anti-fibrillation properties. Y. Sun group successfully obtained CDs by using natural flavonoids compound epigallocatechin as the starting materials, and these CDs could serve as scavenger targeting Alzheimer`s amyloid plaques [37]. V. Guttena group have synthesized CDs using onion waste containing flavonoids compound as precursors and evaluated their application as a fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection in real water and multicolor imaging of Hela cells [38]. The presence of heteroatom within CDs can modify their interactions with protein molecules, which further influences the self-aggregation of protein into amyloid fibrils. For instance, Y. Liu group has reported that CDs with a high oxygen content can act as a high-efficiency inhibitor on human insulin amyloid fibrillation [39]. Hence, it is essential to conduct further investigations into the inhibition abilities of CDs containing different heteroatoms on protein amyloid fibrillation.

Basing on the above discussion, seven CDs, both with and without N-doping, were selected from seven natural and sustainable substances via screening experiments in this study. Specifically, seven CDs were respectively prepared from Protium serratum shell, Poriandrum sativum, Tamarindus indica shell, Peanut shell, Lonicera japonica, Folium perillae and Corn stigma through a one-pot hydrothermal method. Among them, the three CDs derived from Protium serratum shell, Poriandrum sativum, Tamarindus indica shell did not contain N element, while the remaining four CDs all contained N element. The structures of the obtained seven CDs were characterized in detail. The results revealed that the three CDs lacking N element exhibited a higher oxygen content compared to the other four CDs. Subsequently, the effects of these seven CDs on the HL amyloid fibrillation were further investigated. The experimental findings demonstrated that three CDs lacking N element exhibited a higher inhibition ratio on the HL amyloid fibrillation than the other four CDs containing N element. This result could potentially be explained by the high oxygen content in their structures, which was consistent with the results reported in the literature [39]. In addition, these three CDs also capable of scavenging 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH•) radicals and demonstrated low cytotoxicity. This study opened a new pathway for the preparation of multifunctional CDs through green and sustainable chemistry and application as inhibitor on HL amyloid fibrillation.

HL and 8-anilino1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (ANS) were procured from Merck & Co. (St Louis, Mo.). Thioflavin T (ThT) was obtained from J & K Chemicals (Beijing, China). Salicylic acid and DPPH• were purchased from Rhanwn (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) and Thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT) were supplied by Solarbio (Beijing, China). Human neuroblastoma cell line SH-SY5Y was acquired from Qingqi (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was sourced from XP BioMed (Shanghai, China). Protium serratum shell, Coriandrum sativum, Tamarindus indica shell, Peanut shell, Lonicera japonica, Corn stigma and Folium perillae were purchased from the local market.

Seven natural substances (Protium serratum, Coriandrum sativum, Tamarindus indica shell, Peanut shell, Lonicera japonica, Corn stigma and Lolium perillae) were subjected to preliminary treatment prior to further experiment. Firstly, all of these substances were cleaned to remove any surface contaminants and impurities. Subsequently, they were dried in an oven at 60 °C to a constant weight, ensuring complete removal of moisture. The dried substances were then smashed into powder and stored in the dried environment for subsequent usage. The synthetic procedure of the CDs-1 was carried out as follows. Exactly 1 g of Protium serratum shell was weighed and dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water. The resulting mixture was thoroughly stirred for 10 min to ensure homogeneity. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into a hydrothermal reactor and subjected to a reaction at 200 °C for 6 h under sealed conditions. After the reaction was completed, the reacted solution was cooled down to room temperature. The cooled reaction mixture was then centrifuged to separate the solid precipitates from the supernatant. The supernatant was filtered through a filter membrane (0.22 μm) to remove any residual particulate matter. For further purification, the filtrate was further purified using a dialysis bag (500–1000 Da) against deionized water. Finally, the purified solution was freeze-dried to yield the desired CDs-1. The remaining six CDs were prepared following an identical methodology as that of CDs-1. Their specific designations were presented in Table S1 for clear identification and reference.

The prepared CDs were characterized using an array of analytical techniques. Morphology images of the CDs were acquired on transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, USA) with the working voltage at 200 KV. The average hydrodynamic sizes of CDs were measured on a wide-angle dynamic and static laser light scattering meter (DLS) (BI-200SM, Brookhaven, America). Surface element composition of the CDs was probed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Prior to measurement, the sample was carefully prepared in table form and analyzed using a Scientific ESCALAB QXi (Thermo, USA). IR spectra were employed to investigate the chemical bonding within the CDs. The sample was uniformly dispersed in a KBr matrix and analyzed using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). UV–vis spectra of the CDs were obtained using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Analytikjena SPECORD-210, Analy-tikjena, Germany) over a scan range of 200−800 nm. Fluorescence spectra were measured on a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, HITACHI, Japan). The zeta potential of the CDs was determined using a NanoBrook Zetaplus instrument (Brookhaven Instruments, USA). The concentration of the CDs was 50 μg/mL in water. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was performed on an X-ray diffraction spectrometer (D8 ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany) with CuKα as the source and the measurement range was set from 10 to 70 o. Finally, the mean molecular weight of the CDs was measured by MALDI-TOF-MS (Ultraflextremez, Bruker, USA).

The stability of the CDs under different concentration of NaCl solution, different pH and temperature were measured by the changing of fluorescence intensity at maximum excitation wavelength, respectively. The maximum emission wavelength of CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3 were 350 nm, 330 nm and 330 nm, respectively. The final concentration of the CDs was 50 μg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of amyloid fibrils and ThT fluorescence measurement
ce:section.ce:para: To prepared amyloid fibrils, an HL solution was formulated at a concentration of 4 mg/mL in a hydrochloric acid with a pH of 1.4, supplemented with 70 mM NaCl. Subsequently, this solution was heated at 60 °C for 6 days to obtain amyloid fibrils. To explore the impact of the CDs on amyloid fibrillation, varying concentration of CDs were introduced into the reaction system.

For the ThT fluorescence measurement, 60 μL incubating sample was retrieved at different time. These samples were then mixed with 2 mL of ThT (0.01 mM) in phosphate buffered solution. The fluorescence emission intensity of the solution at 486 nm was measured when excitation wavelength was set to 450 nm. For further analyze the data, the normalized ThT fluorescence intensity was fitted using this equation:
(1)

F0 represented the longitudinal intercept of the curve, F represented the ThT fluorescence intensity at t time and t1/2 represented the time when the fluorescence intensity reached half of the maximum fluorescence intensity. rmax represented the maximum rate of the amyloid fibrillation. The data was shown as mean based on three independent experiments.

The inhibition ratio (IR) could be obtained basing on the following equation:
(2)
IR =
(
F 0
− F )
/
F 0
×
100 %
F0 was the ThT fluorescence intensity of HL samples without the CDs and F was the ThT fluorescence intensity with different concentration of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The measurement of the amyloid fibrils morphology
ce:section.ce:para: HL samples after incubating were firstly diluted with water and then a copper grid was dipped into the samples solution for several times. Then, 1% (w/v) phosphotungstic acid was used to stain the samples on the copper grid. The morphology measurement was performed on TEM (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, USA) after drying with the working voltage at 200 kv. Then, the morphology of HL samples on the cleaved mica sheet was further observed on the AFM (NT-MDT, Solver P47, Russia).

HL samples after incubating were firstly diluted 100 times with water. Then, the circular dichroism spectra were measured on a circular dichroism spectrometer (MOS-450 / SFM300, Bio-Logic Company). The scan rate was set to 50 nm/s and the step interval was 0.5 nm.

HL solution was prepared by dissolving HL solid in phosphate buffer solution with a concentration of 200 mM and a pH of 7.4. Subsequently, solution of CDs at varying concentration were blended with HL, with strict control to maintain the final HL concentration at 28 μM. To analyze fluorescence properties, a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, HITACHI, Japan) was employed. The excitation wavelength was set to 280 nm. Excitation slit and emission slit were both adjusted to 10 nm.

To evaluate the antioxidant properties of the CDs, two distinct assays were carried out. Firstly, the scavenging ability of the CDs towards DPPH• was determined. The UV–vis absorbance of DPPH • solution in ethanol (120 μM) were initially measured at 517 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. Then, the CDs were introduced to the DPPH • solution and the mixture was incubated in the dark for 1 h. The change in absorbance at 517 nm was then monitored, providing insight into the scavenging capability of CDs towards DPPH • .

Secondly, the elimination efficiency of the CDs for •OH was investigated. Salicylic acid exhibited a maximum UV–vis absorbance at 520 nm, which increased upon oxidation by •OH. To generate •OH, the reaction between FeSO4 (1.2 mM) and H2O2 (3.3 mM) aqueous solution was employed. After the CDs were introduced to the reacted solution, the absorbance at 520 nm was measured, allowing the calculation of the elimination efficiency for •OH.

The elimination efficiency of the CDs for DPPH • and •OH could be obtained basing on the following equation:
(3)
Elimination %
=
(
A 0
−
A s
)
/
A 0
×
100 %

SH-SY5Y cells were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h in DMEM with FBS. Then, the medium was removed, and the basal medium containing the CDs (2 µg/mL, 4 µg/mL, 8 µg/mL, 16 µg/mL, 32 µg/mL, 64 µg/mL) were added again. After co-incubation for 24 h, 10-μL MTT (5 mg/mL) solution in PBS was added to each well and the system was cultured for an another 4 h. Then, the medium was replaced with 100-μL DMSO. Finally, the optical density (OD) at 570 nm was measured on a microplate reader (INFINTE 200 PRO, Tecan, Switzerland).

Data from 3 independent experiments were all represented as mean ± SD (standard deviation). The 3 individual estimations were statistically analyzed with a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). Statistical significance was expressed as * P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, and *** P < 0.001, ns P > 0.05).

Seven CDs were synthesized using sustainable natural substances (Protium serratum, Coriandrum sativum, Tamarindus indica shell, Peanut shell, Lonicera japonica, Corn stigma and Folium perillae) through straightforward one-pot hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 6 h.

The TEM morphology and HR-TEM images of the CDs-1 were presented in Fig. 1a and 1b, respectively. CDs-1 exhibited a uniform distribution and the average size was 1.78 nm. The lattice spacing was 0.21 nm, corresponding to (100) in-plane lattice spacing of the graphene structure. The morphologies, average size and latter structure of the remaining six CDs were greatly similar to those of CDs-1 and the results were given in supporting information (Fig. S1, Table S1). In addition, the average particle sizes of all seven CDs in aqueous solution were also determined using DLS and the results were shown in Table S1. Obviously, the average particle size of each CDs from DLS measurement was bigger than that from TEM measurement. TEM measurement was usually conducted in static and dry form, but DLS analysis was performed in aqueous solution [40]. The average size obtained from DLS experiment represented the hydrodynamic diameter of CDs in hydrated form. Hence, the size obtained from DLS analysis were slight bigger than that from TEM measurement. This finding aligned with the conclusion reported in existing literature.

XRD and XPS measurements were further carried out to characterize the structure of the obtained CDs. In the XRD spectrum (Fig. 1c) of CDs-1, there was a broad diffraction peak at 22.4 o. The XRD spectra of the remaining six CDs were given in Fig. S2. As depicted in the full XPS spectrum (Fig. 2a ), CDs-1 was composed of C (285 eV, C1s) and O (532 eV, O1s) elements. In the high-resolution spectra of C1s (Fig. 2b), three peaks at 284.9 eV, 286.4 eV and 288.5 eV, corresponding to C = C/CC, CO and C = O, respectively. As displayed in O1s resolution spectra (Fig. 2c), two peaks at 532.10 eV and 533.5 eV were attributed to CO and OC = O, respectively. [41] The full XPS spectra of the remaining six CDs were shown in Fig. S3 and the data were presented in Table S2. In the FT-IR spectrum of CDs-1 (Fig. 2d), three bands at 3400 cm−1, 1409 cm−1, 1087 cm−1 demonstrated the presence of the OH groups. The peak at 1643 cm−1 was corresponded to C = O stretching vibration. The FT-IR of the remaining six CDs were presented in Fig. S4. The analysis results of FT-IR spectra were in accordance with those of XPS, which further indicated that C and O were the main elements of the prepared CDs.
The spectral properties of the CDs were characterized using UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs-1 and the remaining six

CDs were displayed in Fig. 2e and Fig. S6. For CDs-1, the absorption peak at 263 nm was corresponded to the π-π* transition. The fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs-1 at different excitation wavelength were shown in Fig. 2f. The fluorescence emission wavelength was red shifted when the excitation wavelength was changed from 310 nm to 390 nm. The maximum emission intensity was observed at 430 nm with the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. For the other six CDs, the fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. S5) were also obviously dependent on the excitation wavelength. In a word, seven CDs were successfully obtained according to the above analysis.

The stability of the obtained CDs is crucial factor for their applications [42–44]. In this regard, parameters such as ionic concentration, pH and temperature were taken into consideration. The stability of the CDs was investigated by observing the changes in their fluorescence emission intensity. From Fig. 3 (a, d, g) and Fig. 3 (c, f, i), it can be concluded that the normalized fluorescence emission intensity of these three CDs did not change significantly with the increasing of NaCl concentration and temperature. Additionally, the normalized fluorescence emission intensity of these three CDs remained relatively unchanged at a fixed value of pH for 72 h (Fig. 3b, e, h). In a word, these three CDs were all stable at different ionic concentration, pH and temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on amyloid fibrillation
ce:section.ce:para: HL was utilized as model protein to investigate the influence of seven CDs on amyloid fibrillation. ThT can specifically bind with the β-sheet structure in amyloid fibrils and the fluorescence intensity increased greatly after binding. [45] Herein, the ThT fluorescence measurement was performed and the normalized ThT fluorescence intensity of HL samples with the same ratio of the CDs (HL: CDs = 57:1) were shown in Fig. S7. The final inhibition ratio after incubation for 6 days were also calculated and given in Fig. 4a . Among these seven CDs, the inhibition ratio of CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3 were significantly higher than those of the other four CDs. Moreover, CDs- 1 exhibited the highest inhibition ratio. Subsequently, the effects of CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3 on HL amyloid fibrillation were investigated in detail. The effect of the CDs on amyloid fibrillation was greatly correlated to their structural composition. In the existing literature, it was reported that CDs with higher oxygen content may display good inhibition efficiency during the process of amyloid fibrillation. [39,46] Coincidentally, CDs-1 and CDs-3 exhibited a higher oxygen content based on the XPS data analysis (Fig. 4b). In Fig. 4c, CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3 showed a higher peak area attributed to OC = O groups than that of the remaining four CDs. The presence of numerous OC = O groups on CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3 may be likely the main reason for their negative zeta potential (Fig. 4d). In contrast, the other four CDs exhibit positive zeta potential due to the existence of CN and C = N (Fig.4d and Table S2). The disparity in zeta potential may influence the interaction between these CDs and HL protein and further impact their inhibition on amyloid fibrillation.

In Fig. 5a , 5c and 5e, the formation of HL amyloid fibrils contained three stages: the nucleation stage, rapid growth stage and final plateau stage. The normalized ThT fluorescence intensity were all gradually decreased with the increasing of the concentration for CDs-1, CDs-

2 and CDs-3. This demonstrated that all of these three CDs could suppress HL amyloid fibrillation in a dose-dependent manner. The histogram of the final normalized ThT fluorescence intensity and inhibition ratio of the CDs on amyloid fibrillation at different mass ratio were shown in Fig. S8. The inhibition ratio of CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3 reached 92 %, 76 % and 75 % at a mass ratio of 5:1 (HL: CDs), respectively. IC50 referred to the concentration of the CDs when the inhibition ratio was 50 %. Herein, the IC50 values of CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3 were 85.7 μg/mL, 197.7 μg/mL and 207.8 μg/mL, respectively (Fig. 5b, 5d and 5f), which also demonstrated that CDs-1 showed a higher inhibition ratio on HL amyloid fibrillation than CDs-2 and CDs-3. In addition, the ANS fluorescence and intrinsic fluorescence test were also conducted to study the influence of these three CDs on the formation procedure of HL amyloid fibrils. The data were provided in supporting information (Fig. S9, 10). All results were line in with those of ThT fluorescence experiment.

TEM and AFM measurements were employed to observe the morphology of the amyloid fibrils. When HL samples without the CDs were incubated for 6 days, plenty of long and tangled amyloid fibrils were found in both TEM (Fig. 6 a) and AFM (Fig. 6b) images. The same dosage of CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3 (mass ratio of HL: CDs=5:1) was added to the HL samples and the mixed solutions were incubated under the same condition for 6 days. After incubation, only short amyloid fibrils and smaller aggregates were formed. (Fig. 6c, e, g and Fig. 6d, f, h). These results of the TEM and AFM simultaneously demonstrated that all of these three CDs could inhibit the formation of amyloid fibrils.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on protein secondary structure
ce:section.ce:para: During the procedure of amyloid aggregation, the alteration of protein secondary structure can be explored by the measurement of circular dichroism spectra. As depicted in Fig. 7a , b, c, for the HL sample, two negative peaks corresponding to α-helix were found at 208 nm and 222 nm. After being incubated for 6 days, a peak at 212 nm was observed accompanied by the disappearance of these two negative peaks, which clearly proved that α-helix structure has been conversed to β-sheet structure in the procedure of amyloid fibrillation. When HL samples with CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3 were incubated, the circular dichroism spectra were comparable to that of the mature amyloid fibril and the peak intensity at 212 nm was reduced. The contents of secondary structure for the native HL sample, the mature fibril and the HL samples with CDs were calculated and presented in Fig. 7d. The native HL was mainly composed of α-helix with the β-sheet content accounting for 19.8%. Once the amyloid fibril was formed, the β-sheet content rose to 37.3 %. The fact that the β-sheet content in the HL sample with CDs decreased demonstrated that the introduction of the CDs could hinder the process of HL amyloid fibrillation. Moreover, the inhibition capability of the CDs-1 was the most remarkable among them.

The interaction between the protein and the other molecules may cause the change of the microenvironment surrounding the fluorophore. This, in turn, can lead to the quenching of the fluorescence intensity, which can be used as a useful means to investigate the interaction between the other molecules and the protein. [47] The fluorescence emission spectra of HL in presence of CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3 were shown in Fig. S11a, c, e, respectively. The maximum emission peak was located at 340 nm with an excitation wavelength of 280 nm and the fluorescence intensity was significantly quenched with the addition of the CDs. In order to analyze the quenching mechanism, the measured fluorescence intensity should be corrected to eliminate the inner-filter effect basing on this equation:
(4)
F
c o r
=
F
o b s
× e x p
[
(
A
e x
+
A
e m
) / 2
]
where, F cor and F obs represented the corrected and measured fluorescence intensity, respectively. A ex and A em represented the absorbance of the CDs at the excitation and emission wavelength, respectively. The corrected data (Fig. S11b, d, f) continued to decline as the concentration of CDs increased.

Fluorescence quenching mechanism contained dynamic quenching and static quenching. [48] Dynamic quenching involved indirect interaction between the ligand and the protein which occurred through the collision between them. For static quenching, the ligand could interact directly with the protein and form a new complex. To analyze the possible quenching mechanisms, the quenching data were firstly analyzed using the Stern-Volmer equation:
(5)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
S V
Q
where F0 referred to the fluorescence intensity of HL; F referred to the fluorescence intensity of HL with different concentrations of the CDs; Ksv referred to the Stern-Volmer quenching constant; Q referred to the concentration of the CDs. The fitted plot was not a linear curve (Fig. 8a , c, e), which demonstrated that the quenching mechanism of these three CDs were all static quenching rather than dynamic quenching.

The data were further manipulated using the Lineweaver-Burk equation to calculate the binding constant:
(6)
1 /
(
F 0
− F
)
= 1 / F + 1 / Q
F 0
K A

The definitions of F, F0 and Q were the same as that in Eq. (5) and KA represented the binding constant. The fitted curves with longitudinal coordinates of 1/(F0-F) and horizontal ordinates of 1/Q for CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3 were given in Fig. 8b, d, f, respectively. The binding constant KA could be determined from the slope of the curve. KA of CDs-1 (3.50 × 103 L/mol) exceeded that of CDs-2 (3.27 × 103 L/mol) and CDs-3 (2.82 × 103 L/mol), suggesting that the complex formed between CDs-1 and HL was the most stable. The most facile and stable interaction is likely to result in the highest inhibition capability of CDs-1 against amyloid fibrillation.

The measurement of zeta potential was further utilized to investigate the interaction between HL and these three CDs. From Fig. 4d, the zeta potential of these three CDs were all negative. As displayed in Fig. S11g, the zeta potential of the native HL was positive (∼17.5 mV). After the HL was mixed with these three CDs, the zeta potential value of HL was negatively shifted from 17.5 mV to −3.84, −3.27, 3.06 mV for CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3, respectively (Fig. S11g). This result suggested that all of these three CDs underwent electrostatic interaction with positively charged HL [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capability of the CDs to scavenge free radicals
ce:section.ce:para: During the procedure of amyloid fibrillation, additional free radicals are likely to be generated, which can induce oxidative stress. Moreover, an increased number of free radicals could accelerate the progression of amyloid fibrillation. [50] Therefore, scavenging the excessive free radicals may be another effective approach to treat these diseases associated with amyloid fibrillation. Herein, DPPH• was initially chosen to estimate the free radical scavenging ability of CDs, which was straightforward, rapid and cost-effective method for screening the antioxidant prior to performing the cell experiments. In Fig. 9a , c, e, the maximum absorbance of DPPH• at 517 nm was gradually decreased with the increasing concentration of the CDs, which proved that these three CDs could effectively eliminate DPPH•. Furthermore, all of these three CDs demonstrated a dose-dependent ability to scavenge DPPH• with elimination ratio of CDs-1> CDs-3 > CDs-2 at the same concentration (Fig. 9 g, Fig. S12a). As displayed in Fig. S13, the purple color of the DPPH• solution gradually faded away, and the color of the mixture became similar to that of the corresponding CDs with the elevated concentration.

•OH was further utilized to examine the scavenging ability of these three CDs for free radicals. These three CDs also exhibited a dose-dependent scavenging ability toward •OH (Fig. 9b, d, f). However, at the same concentration, the elimination ratio of •OH was lower than that of DPPH• for the same CDs (Fig. 9 h, Fig. S12b). For instance, at a concentration of 700 μg/mL, the elimination ratio of CDs-1 for DPPH• was 93 %, while the elimination ratio of CDs-1 for •OH was merely 37 %

Numerous other reports have been published regarding the synthesis of CDs from bio-materials and their application in antioxidant activity. Herein, the elimination efficiency of the CDs to DPPH• in this work, along with those of some CDs synthesized from bio-materials described in the literature were presented in Table 1 . From the table, elimination efficiency of the CDs-1 to DPPH• was higher than most of the CDs listed in the table.

An assay for measuring neurotoxic effects on the SH-SY5Y cell line was used to determine the biological activity of a potential drug. In this study, the cytotoxicity of CDs in vitro was evaluated by the MTT assay of SH-SY5Y cell. The cell viability of SH-SY5Y cells at different concentrations of three CDs were shown in Fig. S14. The SH-SY5Y cell viability rates were above 80 %, indicating that all the three CDs exhibited low toxicity to SH-SY5Y cell.

Amyloid fibrils rich of β-sheet structures could deposit on the human organs and cause many diseases. The preparation and screening of the inhibitor against the formation of amyloid fibrils was an important overriding concern. In addition, excessive of free radicals could be produced in the procedure of amyloid fibrillation. There have been some studies on the effecting of CDs on amyloid fibrillation, but the reports about multifunction CDs on inhibiting amyloid fibrillation and scavenging free radicals were several. In this work, seven sustainable and green raw materials were screened from numerous natural substance and utilized as precursors to prepare CDs. Significantly, through XPS analysis characterization, it was found that among these seven CDs, three CDs (CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3) did not contain N element, whereas the other four CDs did. The experiments related to amyloid fibrillation demonstrated that three CDs lacking N element exhibited a higher inhibition efficiency compared to the other four CDs containing N element. Coincidentally, the three N-free CDs happened to have a relatively higher oxygen content. Although the relationship between inhibition ability on amyloid fibrillation and the N content within the CDs could not be fully explained, the conclusion that CDs with a high oxygen content act as high-efficiency inhibitors on amyloid fibrillation was consistent with that in the previous report [39]. These three CDs, especially CDs-1, demonstrated good elimination efficiency to DPPH•. The findings have opened up another avenue for the synthesis of CDs from biomaterials carbon source through green synthetic techniques and will assist us in designing CDs with potential application in the biomedicine fields.
